1: 'The apostles and the believers throughout Judea heard that the Gentiles had also received the word of God' — news traveled through the early church via the same networks of travel and communication that served the Roman world: itinerant merchants, imperial post routes, and the movement of pilgrims and travelers between cities. The speed with which the Jerusalem community heard about the Caesarea event reflects both the relatively compact size of the early church and the importance attached to any significant theological development. The reaction ('the circumcised believers took issue with him,' Acts 11:2) demonstrates that the Cornelius event was immediately recognized as a boundary-crossing requiring communal adjudication.
2: 'The circumcised believers' (hoi ek peritomēs — those from/of the circumcision) is the technical designation for Jewish Christians who maintained circumcision as a necessary covenant marker. The same group appears in Galatians 2:12 ('certain men from James... belonged to the circumcision group') and Titus 1:10 ('especially those of the circumcision group'). Their challenge to Peter represents a theological controversy that will persist in the church through Paul's Galatian and Roman letters: whether circumcision remained a requirement for full covenant membership in the messianic community.
3: 'You went into the house of uncircumcised men and ate with them' (eisēlthen pros andras akrobystian echontas kai synephages autois) — the charge specifically focuses on table-fellowship (synephages — you ate together with them) rather than baptism. Entering a Gentile's house incurred ritual impurity according to rabbinic tradition (m. Oholot Acts 18:7 — Gentile dwellings are unclean); sharing a meal with Gentiles violated the purity-maintenance that regular prayer and temple attendance required. The Jerusalem criticism reflects the practical piety concerns of Torah-observant Jewish believers, not merely institutional Pharisaism.
4: 'Starting from the beginning, Peter explained everything to them in order' (kathexēs exetitheto autois) — kathexēs is Luke's characteristic word for orderly sequential narration (Luke 1:3; Acts 3:24; Acts 18:23). Peter's orderly defense before the Jerusalem believers follows the standard form of the Hellenistic defense speech (apologia): chronological presentation of events in the order they occurred, with each element leading logically to the conclusion. Luke's double narration of the Cornelius episode (first in his own voice, Acts 10:1-48; then in Peter's, Acts 11:4-17) is his authentication technique: the story that can be told consistently from two perspectives is reliable.
5: 'I was in the city of Joppa praying, and in a trance I saw a vision' — Peter's first-person account begins from his subjective experience rather than from Cornelius's story (which Luke's narration in Acts 10 had begun with). The personal-experience starting point is the rhetorical choice of someone offering testimony: begin from what you yourself saw and heard, not from the external event. The self-description as in a 'trance' (ekstasei) validates the visionary state's authenticity — Peter is not reporting a dream or a wishful interpretation but an ekstasis, the recognized category of genuine prophetic revelation.
6: The expansion of the animal list in Peter's report ('four-footed animals of the earth, wild beasts, reptiles, and birds') compared to the original account (Acts 10:12 — no 'wild beasts') may reflect either a different tradition, memory expansion in the retelling, or deliberate rhetorical emphasis: by adding 'wild beasts' (thēria — the most obviously 'unclean' category), Peter makes the vision's inclusiveness even more comprehensive in the retelling before the Jerusalem critics. Retelling with slightly expanded detail is a recognized feature of eyewitness accounts in both ancient and modern psychology of memory.
7: 'Kill and eat' — Peter's verbatim repetition of the divine command in testimony is the standard form of divine-command reporting in both OT (Moses reporting God's words to Israel; prophets quoting the divine voice) and Greco-Roman legal contexts (witnesses repeated exact words, not paraphrases). The verbatim divine command, reproduced twice in the narrative (Acts 10:13; Acts 11:7), functions as the unchanging divine word that must be obeyed — its repetition across the double narration confirms that the command was specific, clear, and binding.
8: Peter's reformulation of his refusal in the testimony ('Nothing impure or unclean has ever entered my mouth') uses stoma (mouth) instead of the original ephagon (I ate) — an intensification: the mouth as the entry-point emphasizes the bodily contact with impurity that the refusal was preventing. The switch from 'I have never eaten' (direct, first-person past act) to 'nothing has ever entered my mouth' (categorical, body-centered claim) reflects either normal variation in oral retelling or a deliberate rhetorical choice to make the refusal's totality vivid for the Jerusalem audience who will immediately understand the bodily-purity implications.
9: 'The voice spoke from heaven a second time' — the condensation of the three repetitions (Acts 10:15-16) to 'a second time' in the retelling reflects the rhetorical compression of summary: Peter gives the essential content rather than exhaustive detail. The phōnē ek tou ouranou (voice from heaven) is the bath qol ('daughter of a voice' — the rabbinic term for divine communication through an auditory phenomenon short of direct prophecy; cf. Matthew 3:17; John 12:28; Acts 9:4). The divine voice from heaven carries the authentication weight of direct revelation.
10: The triple repetition and withdrawal to heaven, condensed to 'this happened three times and then it was all pulled back up into heaven' (kai anespasthē palin hapanta eis ton ouranon), receives its confirmation number. Three repetitions of a divine communication in Jewish tradition establish the communication's certainty and binding character (Jonah 1:17 — three days in the fish; the three visions of the book of Daniel; the threefold call to Samuel, 1 Samuel 3:8-10). The immediate withdrawal terminates the vision before interpretation, requiring the recipient to carry the unresolved meaning into encounter with the events that will explain it.
11: 'Right then three men who had been sent to me from Caesarea stopped at the house where I was staying' (kai idou exautēs treis andres epestēsan epi tēn oikian) — kai idou (and behold!) is Luke's particle of providential coincidence: the unexpected event that reveals divine coordination behind the surface of simultaneity. Peter's use of exautēs (immediately, right then) emphasizes the precision of the timing: not 'later that day' but at the exact moment the vision's perplexity was still unresolved. The Cornelius messengers' arrival as the divine explanation of the unresolved vision is the interpretive key Peter offers to the Jerusalem critics.
12: 'These six brothers also went with me' — Peter's identification of the six witnesses by their role as accompanying witnesses ('six brothers') rather than by name provides the legal framework for his testimony: Deuteronomy 19:15's requirement of two or three witnesses is exceeded. The six Jewish-Christian witnesses to the Cornelius event constitute a super-majority witness-testimony that can be cross-examined by the Jerusalem church. Luke consistently documents the witness-structures that give apostolic events their legal standing (Acts 1:21-22; Acts 2:32; Acts 3:15; Acts 5:32; Acts 10:39-41).
13: The chain of reporting that Peter rehearses — angel to Cornelius → Cornelius to Peter's messengers → messengers to Peter → Peter to Jerusalem — constitutes a multi-link chain of testimony. Each link in the chain is a separate witness to the divine initiation of the event. Luke's interest in multiple-corroborated testimony reflects both his historiographical method (Luke 1:1-4 — 'handed down to us by those who from the first were eyewitnesses') and the early church's theological need to demonstrate that the inclusion of Gentiles was not a human innovation but a divinely initiated event with multiple independent witnesses.
14: 'He will bring you a message through which you and all your household will be saved' (rhēmata di' hōn sōthēsē su kai pas ho oikos sou) — the household-salvation scope is the distinctive unit of Cornelius's conversion: not merely Cornelius as an individual but his entire household. This matches the pattern throughout Acts: Lydia and her household (Acts 16:15), the Philippian jailer and his household (Acts 16:34), Crispus and his household (Acts 18:8). The household as the primary social unit of ancient Mediterranean life becomes the primary unit of the gospel's reception — the conversion of a household-head brought his entire social and domestic network into the community.
15: 'As I began to speak, the Holy Spirit came on them as he had come on us at the beginning' (en archē — at the beginning) — Peter's reference to 'the beginning' situates the Cornelius Pentecost in relation to the Jerusalem Pentecost as the normative event. The phrase en archē echoes John 1:1 ('In the beginning was the Word') and Genesis 1:1 ('In the beginning God created') — both primordial starting points. The Cornelius event is measured against the foundational Pentecost as its extension: the same Spirit, the same pattern, the same beginning repeated in a new location with a new people.
16: 'John baptized with water, but you will be baptized with the Holy Spirit' (Iōannēs men ebaptisen hydati, hymeis de baptisthēsesthe en pneumati hagiō) — the Lord's prophetic word recalled by Peter is from Acts 1:5 (cf. Luke 3:16; Matthew 3:11; Mark 1:8). The contrast water/Spirit is the Baptist's own formulation: his preparatory water-baptism pointed forward to the messianic Spirit-baptism. Peter's recollection at this moment — 'I remembered the Lord's words' (emnēsthēn de tou rhēmatos Kyriou) — is the interpretive breakthrough: the Cornelius event is the fulfillment of a specific prophetic word that defines what Spirit-baptism means and who receives it.
17: 'So if God gave them the same gift he gave us when we believed in the Lord Jesus Christ, who was I to think that I could oppose God?' — the rhetorical question (egō tis ēmēn dynatos kōlysai ton theon — who was I to be able to hinder God?) positions Peter as the potential obstacle: the human barrier that could have frustrated divine intention. The same kōlyō (to hinder, prevent) that Peter had used rhetorically at the baptism ('can anyone hinder the water?', Acts 10:47) is now applied to the hypothetical of hindering God. The theological conclusion is irresistible: if God initiated, God authenticated, and God gave the same gift — to resist would be theomachein (fighting against God).
18: 'So then, even to Gentiles God has granted repentance that leads to life' (ara kai tois ethnesin ho theos tēn metanoian eis zōēn edōken) — the Jerusalem church's conclusion is the first formal ecclesial recognition of Gentile inclusion without circumcision. Metanoia (repentance) as God's gift (edōken — has given) rather than human achievement reflects the consistent Acts theology: even the turning-toward-God is God's grant, not human initiation (Acts 5:31 — 'God exalted him... to give repentance and forgiveness of sins to Israel'). The extension of the same gift to Gentiles completes the universalization of what was first given to Israel.
19: 'Those who had been scattered by the persecution that broke out when Stephen was killed' — the connection back to Stephen's death (Acts 7:54-8:3) creates a narrative arc: the persecution that scattered the Jerusalem community (Acts 8:1 — 'they were all scattered throughout Judea and Samaria') becomes the mechanism of the mission's geographic expansion. Phoenicia (the Lebanese coast), Cyprus (Barnabas's homeland, Acts 4:36), and Antioch represent the three primary destinations of the Stephen-scattered believers — all significant diaspora centers with established Jewish communities and synagogues that served as the initial mission contexts.
20: 'Men from Cyprus and Cyrene went to Antioch and began to speak to Greeks also' (Hellēnas — Greeks, not Hellēnistai/Greek-speaking Jews) — the Antioch breakthrough differs from Cornelius in its agents (unnamed Cypriot and Cyrenian missionaries, not apostles) and its context (an urban Gentile population in the empire's third-largest city, not a God-fearing Roman centurion). Antioch (Syrian Antioch, modern Antakya/Antiochia on the Orontes) was a city of approximately half a million people, with a large and established Jewish community. The missionaries' transition from preaching to Hellenist Jews to preaching to ethnic Greeks (Hellēnas) is the sociological watershed for Gentile-Christianity's development.
21: 'The Lord's hand was with them' (cheir Kyriou ēn met' autōn) — the idiom 'hand of the Lord' (cheir Kyriou) is the OT power-formula for divine intervention in history: Exodus 9:3; Judges 2:15; 1 Samuel 5:6, 11; 2 Samuel 24:14; 1 Kings 18:46; 2 Kings 3:15; Isaiah 66:14; Ezekiel 1:3. Luke's application of this OT divine-power formula to the Antioch mission validates it as genuine divine action: the unnamed missionaries from Cyprus and Cyrene are as much instruments of the divine 'hand' as Moses was at the Exodus.
22: 'The church at Jerusalem sent Barnabas to Antioch' — Barnabas's appointment as Jerusalem's investigative representative to the Antioch mission reflects the Jerusalem church's continuing sense of responsibility for the wider mission and its authority to validate or correct significant developments. Barnabas (Acts 4:36 — a Levite from Cyprus) is ideally suited: as a Cypriot himself, he shares the cultural background of the Cyprus-origin missionaries. His appointment over a Jerusalem-born Jewish Christian reflects either his reputation for cross-cultural mediation or Luke's awareness that his Cypriot origin made him the natural choice for evaluating a Cyprus-initiated mission.
23: 'He saw what the grace of God had done' (idōn tēn charin tēn tou theou) — the phrase identifies divine grace as visually observable: Barnabas sees something and identifies it as the grace of God. What he sees is presumably the community life, the transformed behavior, and the mixed-ethnicity fellowship of the Antioch believers. 'He was glad' (echarē) — his reaction is joy, not suspicion or correction. Unlike some circumcision-party critics, Barnabas accepts the evidence of grace in a Gentile-inclusive community. His encouragement to 'remain true to the Lord with all their hearts' is pastoral consolidation, not theological adjudication.
24: 'A good man, full of the Holy Spirit and faith' — Luke's character-summary of Barnabas echoes his summaries of other key figures: Stephen (Acts 6:5, 8 — full of faith and the Holy Spirit, full of grace and power), the Seven (Acts 6:3 — full of the Spirit and wisdom). The combination of 'good man' (anēr agathos) with pneumatological fullness and faith is Luke's profile of an effective missionary leader. 'A great number of people were brought to the Lord' (prosetethe ochlos hikanos tō Kyriō) — prosetethe (was added, passive — God does the adding) follows the Pentecost pattern (Acts 2:41, 47 — 'the Lord added to their number daily').
25: 'Then Barnabas went to Tarsus to look for Saul' (anazētein Saulon — to search up/out Saul) — Tarsus was Paul's home city (Acts 9:11; Acts 21:39; Acts 22:3), the capital of Cilicia on the Aegean-Mediterranean coast of modern Turkey, and a significant intellectual center (Strabo described it as surpassing Athens and Alexandria in philosophical culture). After his Damascus experience (Acts 9:1-25) and Jerusalem visit (Acts 9:26-29), Paul had been sent to Tarsus (Acts 9:30 — 'the believers sent him to Tarsus'). The period between Paul's Tarsus departure and Barnabas's recruitment — perhaps 5-7 years — is largely unaccounted for in Acts (cf. Galatians 1:21-2:1 — Paul in Syria and Cilicia).
26: 'The disciples were first called Christians (Christianous) at Antioch' — the name Christianos (one belonging to Christus/Christ) is a Latin-style formation using the -ianus suffix that designated adherents or partisans of a leader (Pompeianus = Pompey's man; Herodianus = Herod's man). The name was apparently given by outsiders — Antioch's Greek-speaking Gentile population would have recognized the movement's focus on 'the Christ' and designated its members accordingly. Luke's note that the name originated in Antioch (not Jerusalem) places the first distinctively Gentile-identified label for the movement in the city where Gentile Christianity first took organizational form.
27: 'Some prophets came down from Jerusalem to Antioch' — the prophets (prophētai) who traveled from Jerusalem to Antioch represent a mobile charismatic ministry distinct from the settled leadership of local churches (elders, teachers). Acts mentions prophets repeatedly: Agabus (Acts 11:28; Acts 21:10), Philip's four daughters (Acts 21:9), Barnabas, Simeon Niger, Lucius, Manaen, and Saul (Acts 13:1). The Didache (Acts 11:3-12) provides procedures for evaluating itinerant prophets, suggesting this was a significant and sometimes problematic feature of early church life. The Jerusalem-to-Antioch direction of prophetic travel reflects Jerusalem's continued role as the movement's spiritual capital.
28: Agabus's famine prophecy (esēmanen dia tou pneumatos limon megan mellein eseshai eph' holēn tēn oikoumenēn — signified through the Spirit that a great famine was about to come upon the whole inhabited world) — the word sēmainō (to signify, indicate) is John's vocabulary for prophetic sign-speech (John 12:33; Acts 18:32; Acts 21:19; Revelation 1:1). The famine 'over the entire Roman world' (oikoumenē — the inhabited world, standard Roman geographical designation for the empire) was not a single global event but a series of regional famines under Claudius (41-54 CE). Josephus describes a severe famine in Palestine under procurators Fadus and Alexander (ca. 46-48 CE; Ant. 20.5.2). Luke's editorial note ('this happened during the reign of Claudius') is one of Acts' explicit historical synchronizations.
29: 'The disciples, as each one was able, decided to provide help for the brothers and sisters living in Judea' (kathōs ēuporeitō tis — according as each one was prospering/had the means) — the collection's ability-proportioned structure ('as each one was able') corresponds to Paul's later instructions about the Corinthian collection (1 Corinthians 16:2 — 'each of you is to put something aside and store it up, as he may prosper'; 2 Corinthians 8:12 — 'it is acceptable according to what a person has'). The Antioch relief collection is the first inter-church financial solidarity action in Acts — Gentile-majority Antioch supporting the Jewish-majority Jerusalem community, reversing the expected direction of cultural dependency.
30: 'Sending their gift to the elders by Barnabas and Saul' — the 'elders' (presbyterous) receiving the relief collection represent the leadership structure that has emerged in Jerusalem alongside (or replacing) the apostolic oversight of the early chapters. The term presbyteros (elder) derives from the Jewish synagogue's elder-board (zqenim) and appears here for the first time in Acts as a Jerusalem leadership designation. Paul and Barnabas's joint delivery of the relief gift marks the first explicit collaboration of these two figures — the partnership that will characterize the first missionary journey (Acts 13-14).
